timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Fall of Jerusalem

sections.header: Verses 1-20 -- "Babylon Is Taken, Bel Is Put to Shame"

heb.paragraph: "Babylon will be captured; Bel (bel) will be put to shame, Marduk filled with terror" (v.2). Bel ("lord") is the title of Marduk, Babylon's chief deity. The fall of Babylon means the fall of Marduk -- the most powerful god in the ancient Near Eastern pantheon defeated by the God who has "no form." The contest between YHWH and Marduk underlies the entire Babylon oracle.

refs.note: Isaiah's parallel oracle against Babylon -- the prophetic tradition of condemning the empire.
Revelation's "Babylon the Great" draws directly on Jeremiah 50-51 for its imagery of the fallen city.
The handwriting on the wall: Belshazzar's feast and Babylon's fall to Persia -- the historical fulfillment.

notes.note: Bel and Marduk humiliated -- the theological heart of the oracle. Babylon's gods are not merely defeated but "put to shame." Their impotence is exposed.
As Babylon falls, Israel and Judah together seek the LORD, asking "the way to Zion." Babylon's destruction enables Israel's restoration -- the two events are theologically linked.
"My people have been lost sheep; their shepherds have led them astray." The shepherd metaphor returns: Israel's own leaders caused the exile, and Babylon is culpable for devouring the flock.

"Israel is a scattered flock." Assyria devoured first, Babylon finished the job. But God will punish Babylon as he punished Assyria, and "the guilt of Israel will be searched for but there will be none" -- complete forgiveness.

Calvin reads Israel's restoration as the flip side of Babylon's fall: "God never judges one party without simultaneously delivering another. Destruction and salvation are two faces of the same sovereign act."
Marduk was the patron deity of Babylon, celebrated in the Enuma Elish creation epic as the supreme god who defeated Tiamat. His humiliation represents the collapse of Babylonian theological claims.
"An alliance of great nations from the land of the north" -- the Medo-Persian coalition that conquered Babylon in 539 BC under Cyrus the Great.

Lundbom identifies the Babylon oracle as "the theological culmination of the book." The servant of God (25:9) becomes the enemy of God. The instrument of judgment becomes the object of judgment. The reversal is total.

Brueggemann reads the fall of Marduk as "the deconstruction of imperial religion." Every empire claims divine sponsorship; the fall of the empire exposes the claim as fraud. Marduk's shame is the shame of every ideology that sacralizes power.

hist.context: Chapters 50-51 form the climactic oracle of the book -- the judgment on Babylon itself. The instrument of God's judgment against Judah now faces its own judgment. The oracle is the longest sustained poem in Jeremiah (110 verses) and serves as the theological counterweight to chapters 2-25: as Judah was judged for abandoning God, Babylon is judged for its arrogance, cruelty, and opposition to God's people. Israel's restoration is woven throughout: as Babylon falls, Israel rises.

heb.paragraph: "Attack the land of Merathaim (double rebellion) and those who live in Pekod (punishment)" (v.21). Both names are wordplays: Merathaim puns on southern Babylonia's marsh region (mat marrati) while meaning "double rebellion," and Pekod puns on the Puqudu tribe while meaning "punishment." The geography encodes the theology.

refs.note: Isaiah's parallel "virgin daughter of Babylon" oracle -- the pampered empire humiliated.
"Fallen! Fallen is Babylon the Great!" -- John echoes the prophetic tradition of announcing Babylon's definitive collapse.

notes.note: "How broken and shattered is the hammer of the whole earth!" Babylon, which shattered nations, is now itself shattered. The hammer meets the anvil of divine judgment.
"The LORD has opened his arsenal." The military metaphor portrays God as a warrior-king deploying his weapons. Babylon faces not merely a human enemy but the divine arsenal.
"Israel and Judah are oppressed ... but their Redeemer is strong." The go'el (kinsman-redeemer) of Israel will plead their case and bring rest to the land.
"Like Sodom and Gomorrah" -- Babylon's fate will mirror the quintessential cities of divine destruction. The comparison is total annihilation.

Calvin celebrates the Redeemer title: "Israel has a go'el -- a kinsman who is obligated to act on their behalf. God assumes the family obligation of redemption. The enslaved have a powerful relative."
Merathaim may be a play on Babylonian mat marrati, the "bitter river" region of southern Mesopotamia, combined with the Hebrew meaning "double rebellion."
"A drought on her waters!" -- Babylon's famous canal system and the Euphrates were its economic lifeline. Cutting the water supply was a strategic military tactic.

Lundbom reads the sustained length of the Babylon oracle as "proportional justice." Babylon received the longest judgment because it inflicted the most damage. The scale of the oracle matches the scale of the crime.
Brueggemann identifies the Redeemer oracle as "the theological heart of the Babylon collection." God is not a neutral arbiter but a committed kinsman who takes Israel's side against the oppressor.

hist.context: The second half escalates the rhetoric. Babylon is called "the hammer of the whole earth" (v.23) -- now itself broken. God opens his "arsenal" (v.25) and brings out "the weapons of his wrath." The oracle alternates between Babylon's destruction and Israel's release, weaving judgment and salvation into a single fabric. The Redeemer (go'el) is invoked: "their Redeemer is strong; the LORD Almighty is his name" (v.34).

rows.label: vv.11-16
rows.text: Babylon Judged for Plundering God's Heritage

lit.note: The first half of the climactic oracle. Babylon's destruction and Israel's restoration are woven together as two sides of one divine act.

themes.note: Judgment and Sovereignty both at 10 -- the supreme act of divine power against the supreme human empire. Mercy at 6: Israel's restoration within the judgment oracle.

The contest between YHWH and Marduk underlies the entire Babylon oracle.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Oracle Against Babylon (Part 1)

translations.text: “Announce and proclaim among the nations, lift up a banner and proclaim it; keep nothing back, but say, ‘Babylon will be captured; Bel will be put to shame, Marduk filled with terror. Her images will be put to shame and her idols filled with terror.’

“Declare among the nations and proclaim, set up a banner and proclaim, conceal it not, and say: ‘Babylon is taken, Bel is put to shame, Merodach is dismayed. Her images are put to shame, her idols are dismayed.’
A nation from the north will attack her and lay waste her land. No one will live in it; both people and animals will flee away.
“For out of the north a nation has come up against her, which shall make her land a desolation, and none shall dwell in it; both man and beast shall flee away.

thread.text: Isaiah's parallel oracle against Babylon -- the prophetic tradition of condemning the empire.
Revelation's "Babylon the Great" draws directly on Jeremiah 50-51 for its imagery of the fallen city.
The handwriting on the wall: Belshazzar's feast and Babylon's fall to Persia -- the historical fulfillment.
Isaiah's parallel "virgin daughter of Babylon" oracle -- the pampered empire humiliated.
"Fallen! Fallen is Babylon the Great!" -- John echoes the prophetic tradition of announcing Babylon's definitive collapse.

coaching.tip: The Babylon oracle is the longest in Jeremiah (two chapters, 50–51) — appropriate since Babylon is both God's instrument and the ultimate object of judgment. The empire that executed God's verdict on Judah will itself face God's verdict. Jeremiah's theology refuses to let any human power claim ultimate authority. The tool doesn't become the toolmaker.

'In those days, at that time, the people of Israel and Judah together will go in tears to seek the LORD' (v. 4). Embedded in the Babylon oracle is a reunion prophecy — north and south together, seeking God with weeping. Jeremiah's Babylon chapters keep pivoting from judgment on the empire to restoration of the people. The fall of the oppressor and the homecoming of the oppressed are one event seen from two sides.
